question_title: What percentage of the vote will Nigel Farage receive in the Clacton parliamentary by-election in August 2026?
summary: Farage received 46.2% in Clacton in the 2024 GE against full competition (Con 27.9%, Lab 16.2%, others). For this by-election, all major parties (Con, Lab, LibDem, Green, Restore Britain) are boycotting, leaving only novelty/independent candidates (Count Binface as nominal "main challenger") across a 12-15 candidate field that splits the residual anti-Farage vote many ways. Reform is nationally much stronger now (25-27% vs 14% in 2024), Clacton is a strong Leave/Reform seat, and differential turnout in a boycotted "vanity" contest favors the energized Reform base. This lifts Farage's share well above his 2024 baseline. Center ~64%. Downside risk: intense national anti-Farage sentiment (33% prefer Binface, 52% say don't re-elect) could mobilize protest turnout and consolidate on Binface (Bercow 2010 precedent held a boycotted incumbent near 47%). No constituency-level poll exists, so genuine uncertainty is wide. Scenario-mixture Monte Carlo (55% base ~64, 25% landslide ~73, 20% embarrassment ~55).

factors.description: All five major parties boycott; ~54% of the previously-split serious opposition vote removed from a strong Leave/Reform seat
12-15 mostly novelty/independent candidates split the anti-Farage vote, no credible single challenger to consolidate opposition
Differential turnout favors energized Reform base; Reform national polling 25-27% vs 14% in 2024
Downside: intense national anti-Farage sentiment could mobilize protest turnout for Binface (Bercow 2010 held boycotted incumbent near 47%)

reasoning:
Forecast submitted. 

**Summary:** For the Clacton by-election (13 Aug 2026), I forecast Farage's Reform UK vote share centered at **~64%** (P10 ~54.5%, P50 ~64.4%, P90 ~74.4%).

**Key reasoning:**
- Farage won 46.2% in 2024 against full competition. Now all five major parties (Conservative, Labour, Lib Dem, Green, Restore Britain) are boycotting, removing ~54% of the previously-split serious opposition.
- The remaining field is 12-15 novelty/independent candidates (Count Binface as nominal "main challenger"), which fragments the anti-Farage vote and inflates his percentage.
- Reform is nationally much stronger now (25-27% vs 14% in 2024), Clacton is a strong Leave/Reform seat, and differential turnout in a boycotted contest favors his energized base.
- Downside risk (kept the lower tail wide): intense national anti-Farage sentiment could mobilize protest turnout for Binface — the Bercow 2010 precedent shows a boycotted incumbent can be held near 47%. No constituency-level poll exists, so uncertainty is genuinely wide.

The premortem reviewer approved the forecast.
resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as the percentage vote share received by the Reform UK candidate Nigel Farage in the Clacton parliamentary by-election held on August 13, 2026, as declared by [Tendring District Council](https://www.tendringdc.gov.uk/content/parliamentary-results) Returning Officer. The percentage refers to the share of valid votes cast for the Reform UK candidate, consistent with standard reporting conventions used by [The Electoral Commission](https://www.electoralcommission.org.uk/our-guidance).
fine_print:
If the by-election is postponed or cancelled such that no result is declared on or before September 3, 2026, this question will be **annulled**. Otherwise, it will resolve according to the results of the postponed election.

***
This question's information (resolution criteria, fine print, background info, etc) is synced with an [original identical question](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44551) which opened on 2026-07-13 17:00:00. This question will resolve based on the resolution criteria and fine print of the linked original question. However, if this question would resolve differently than the original question, then this question will be annulled. Additionally, if the original question's resolution could have been known before this question opened, then this question will be annulled.

factors.description: Farage got 46.2% in 2024 GE with full competition; all major parties (Con, Lab, LibDem, Green, Restore) now boycotting, removing ~54% of the split opposition vote
12-15 mostly novelty/independent candidates split the anti-Farage vote many ways, no credible single challenger to consolidate opposition (helps Farage %)
Differential turnout: boycotted 'vanity' by-election demoralizes opposition, energizes Reform base; Reform national polling up to 25-27% from 14% in 2024
Precedent: Bercow Speaker 2010 got only ~47% when major parties stood aside but a credible independent ran; high national anti-Farage sentiment (33% prefer Binface) could channel protest turnout

input.anchor: Farage 2024 baseline 46.2%; adjusted upward for boycott of all major parties and stronger Reform position -> center ~64%
input.assessment: Farage in a Clacton by-election where all major parties boycott. Base 46.2% (2024) rises substantially because ~54% of the previously-split opposition vote is removed and no credible challenger exists—only Count Binface and novelty/independent candidates. Reform is nationally stronger now. Differential turnout favors the energized Reform base. Center ~64%. Downside risk: national anti-Farage sentiment mobilizes protest turnout for Binface (Bercow 2010 precedent shows even boycotted incumbents can be held near 47%), but Clacton is a strong Leave/Reform area unlike national sentiment. Wide distribution given no constituency poll exists. Bounds 45-85 open both ends; my P1 ~47, P99 ~81.
input.key_uncertainties: Turnout composition and whether national anti-Farage sentiment translates into local protest votes for Count Binface. No constituency poll exists.
input.tool_audit: research() returned rich cross-validated data (PollCheck, Democracy Club, Wikipedia, Ipsos). No constituency-level poll exists. execute_code ran scenario mixture Monte Carlo cleanly.
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well after fixing the input schema (needs 'questions' array). No by-election-specific polling exists, so this is inherently a scenario-judgment problem rather than a data-driven one. A tool for historical boycotted/uncontested by-election vote shares would have helped calibrate the incumbent-dominance base rate.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I verified all four factors against the research trace and ran an independent check on the one un-researched claim.

GROUNDING — all core claims supported:
- Factor 1 (46.2% 2024 baseline; 5-party boycott: Con, Lab, LibDem, Green, Restore): directly confirmed by PollCheck, Wikipedia, and electionresults.uk in the trace.
- Factor 2 (12–15 mostly novelty/independent candidates, no credible single challenger): confirmed by Democracy Club (12 named) and PollCheck (~15 intended); Count Binface is the only "main challenger" and is a novelty candidate.
- Factor 3 (Reform national polling 25–27%, up from ~14% in 2024): confirmed by multiple YouGov polls and 2026 local election projections in the trace.
- Factor 4 (Bercow 2010 precedent, ~47% when parties stood aside): This is the ONLY claim not in the research trace — no Bercow search was run. I independently verified it: Bercow won Buckingham 2010 with a 25.9% majority and a vote share in the high-40s, so "~47%" is roughly accurate as base-rate color. It is used correctly as DOWNSIDE context with a negative logit (−0.8), so it does not inflate the forecast. Minor unsupported detail, not a forecast-moving hallucination.

NO double-counting: Factors 1–3 rest on distinct evidence (baseline+boycott / ballot fragmentation / national polling+turnout); Factor 4 is the counterweight.

LOGIT SIGNS all match descriptions; Factor 4 correctly pulls down.

RESOLUTION CRITERIA fully provided and engaged with (Reform candidate's share of valid votes). No recovery needed. Annulment/postponement fine print does not affect the numeric estimate.

NO pre-publication event (election Aug 13, 2026 is after the July 13 publish date; forecast is forward-looking).

NO regime-spanning data issue — the Monte Carlo is a hand-specified scenario mixture (55% base ~64, 25% landslide ~73, 20% embarrassment ~55), not drift/volatility estimated from a contaminated historical window.

PROBABILITY: Center 64% is well-supported. Removing ~54% of the previously-split serious opposition from a strong Leave/Reform seat, with only novelty candidates remaining and Reform nationally stronger, plausibly lifts Farage well above his 46.2% baseline. The distribution retains a sensible downside tail (P1 ~47.5, capturing Binface protest consolidation) and upside tail (P99 ~81). My independent estimate lands in the same region (~62–68%), so no meaningful divergence.

COUNTERARGUMENT is genuinely two-sided and constructs a coherent alternative "embarrassment" story (Binface consolidation, Bercow precedent, 52% national anti-re-election sentiment) rather than restating the conclusion — a real attempt to self-break.

SELF-CONFIDENCE (0.60) is well-calibrated given no constituency-level poll exists; the agent appropriately flags this as the core uncertainty.

TOOL AUDIT: The two research failures in the ground-truth metrics were pydantic schema-validation errors (missing "questions" field) that the agent immediately corrected on its third call; the substantive research succeeded. The agent's narrative ("rich cross-validated data," AskNews unavailable) does not falsely claim zero failures, so no misrepresentation.

Only trivial issue (un-researched but accurate, correctly-directioned Bercow precedent). Approve.
